blocks:
Ayah

بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (۱)
In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (1)

Key Points

Among different nations and peoples, it is customary to begin important and valuable tasks in the name of a great figure whom they respect and love, so that the work may be auspicious and successful. Of course, they act based on their correct or corrupt beliefs. Sometimes they start the work in the name of idols and tyrants, and sometimes in the Name of Allah and by the hands of Allah's saints. Just as in the Battle of the Trench (Khandaq), the Messenger of Allah صلى الله علیه وآله struck the first pickaxe into the ground.(7)

«بسم اللَّه الرّحمن الرّحیم» is the beginning of the Divine Book. «بسم اللَّه» was not only at the beginning of the Quran but also at the beginning of all heavenly books. «بسم اللَّه» was placed at the forefront of the work and actions of all Prophets. When the Ark of Noah عليه السلام set sail amidst the waves of the flood, Noah عليه السلام told his companions: Embark, for «بسم اللَّه مجراها و مرساها»(8) meaning the movement and stopping of this Ark is in the Name of Allah. Prophet Solomon عليه السلام also began his invitation letter with the phrase «بسم اللَّه الرّحمن الرّحیم»(9) when he called the Queen of Sheba to faith.

Imam Ali عليه السلام said: «بسم اللَّه» is the source of blessing for deeds, and abandoning it leads to failure. Furthermore, he told a person who was writing the phrase «بسم اللَّه»: «جَوِّدها» Write it beautifully.(10)

Uttering «بسم اللَّه» is recommended at the start of every task; in eating, sleeping, writing, mounting a conveyance, traveling, and many other activities. Even if an animal is slaughtered without mentioning the Name of Allah, consuming its meat is forbidden (Haram). This symbolizes that the food of purposeful and monotheistic humans must also have a Divine orientation.

We read in a Hadith: Do not forget «بسم اللَّه», even when writing a single couplet of poetry. And narrations have been reported regarding the reward for the person who first teaches «بسم اللَّه» to a child.(11)

Question: Why is «بسم اللَّه» recommended at the start of every task?

Answer: «بسم اللَّه» is the emblem and sign of a Muslim, and all his actions must have a Divine color. Just as the products and goods manufactured by a factory bear the emblem and mark of that factory, whether in partial or general form. For example, a china factory places its mark on all dishes, whether they are large or small. Or, the flag of every country is flown over its government offices, schools, and barracks, as well as over its ships at sea, and placed on the administrative desks of its employees.

Question: Is «بسم اللَّه الرّحمن الرّحیم» an independent Ayah?

Answer: According to the belief of the Ahl al-Bayt of the Messenger of Allah علیهم السلام, who precede other jurisprudential leaders of the schools of thought by a hundred years, who were martyred in the way of Allah, and whose infallibility and purity are explicitly stated in the Quran, the Ayah «بسم اللَّه الرّحمن الرّحیم» is an independent Ayah and part of the Quran. Fakhr Razi, in his commentary, provides sixteen reasons why «بسم اللَّه» is part of the Surah. Alusi also holds this belief. In Musnad Ahmad, «بسم اللَّه» is also counted as part of the Surah.(12) Some individuals who did not consider Bismillah part of the Surah or omitted it in prayer were met with objection. It is stated in Mustadrak al-Hakim: One day Mu'awiyah did not say «بسم اللَّه» in prayer, and the people protested to him, saying: «أسرقت أم نَیستَ», Did you steal the Ayah or forget it?! (13)

The infallible Imams علیهم السلام insisted on reciting Bismillah aloud in prayer. Imam Baqir عليه السلام, regarding those who did not recite «بسم اللَّه» in prayer or did not count it as part of the Surah, used to say: «سَرقوا اكرم آیة»(14) They stole the most honored Ayah of the Quran. In Sunan al-Bayhaqi, it is mentioned in a Hadith: Why have some people not included «بسم اللَّه» as part of the Surah!(15)

Shahid Mutahhari (may his soul be sanctified) in his commentary on Surah Al-Fatiha, identifies Ibn Abbas, Asim, Kisa'i, Ibn Umar, Ibn Zubayr, Ata, Ibn Tawus, Fakhr Razi, and Suyuti as among those who considered Bismillah part of the Surah.

In Tafsir Qurtubi, it is narrated from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام: «بسم اللَّه» is the crown of the Surahs. «بسم اللَّه» is omitted only at the beginning of Surah Bara'ah (Surah At-Tawbah), and this, according to the statement of Imam Ali عليه السلام, is because «بسم اللَّه» is a word of security and mercy, and declaring dissociation (Bara'ah) from the disbelievers and polytheists is incompatible with expressing love and mercy.(16)

Characteristics of «بسم اللّه»

  1. «بسم اللَّه» indicates the Divine color and hue, and expresses our monotheistic orientation.(17)
  2. «بسم اللَّه» is the symbol of Monotheism (Tawhid), while mentioning the names of others instead of it is the symbol of disbelief (Kufr), and associating the Name of Allah with the names of others is a sign of polytheism (Shirk). We should neither mention another name alongside the Name of Allah nor in place of His Name.(18)
  3. «بسم اللَّه» is the symbol of permanence and duration. Because whatever does not have a Divine color is perishable.(19)
  4. «بسم اللَّه» is the symbol of love for Allah and reliance upon Him. We love Him Who is the Most Compassionate (Rahman) and the Most Merciful (Rahim), and we begin our work by relying upon Him, for mentioning His Name attracts mercy.
  5. «بسم اللَّه» is the symbol of exiting arrogance and expressing humility before the Divine Threshold.
  6. «بسم اللَّه» is the first step on the path of servitude and worship.
  7. «بسم اللَّه» causes Satan to flee. Whoever has Allah accompanying him, Satan cannot affect him.
  8. «بسم اللَّه» is the factor that sanctifies deeds and insures them.
  9. «بسم اللَّه» is the remembrance of Allah, meaning, O Allah! I have not forgotten You.
  10. «بسم اللَّه» expresses our motivation, meaning, O Allah, my goal is You, not people, not tyrants, not worldly attractions, and not desires.
  11. Imam Reza عليه السلام said: «بسم اللَّه» is closer to the Greatest Name of Allah (Ism al-A'zam) than the blackness of the eye is to its whiteness.(20) 7) Bihar, Vol. 20, p. 218. 8) Hud, 41. 9) An-Naml, 30. 10) Kanz al-Ummal, H. 29558. 11) Tafsir Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 43. 12) Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 3, p. 177 and Vol. 4, p. 85. 13) Mustadrak, Vol. 3, p. 233. 14) Bihar, Vol. 85, p. 20. 15) Sunan al-Bayhaqi, Vol. 2, p. 50. 16) Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Fakhr Razi. 17) Imam Reza عليه السلام says: Bismillah means I place the sign of servitude to Allah upon myself. Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn. 18) Not only His Essence, but His Name is also free from any partner; «سبّح اسم ربّك الاعلى» Even starting a task in the name of Allah and Muhammad صلى الله علیه وآله is forbidden. Ithbat al-Hudat, Vol. 7, p. 482. 19) «كلّ شى ء هالك الاّ وجهه» Al-Qasas, 88. 20) Tafsir Rahnuma.

Messages

  1. «بسم اللَّه الرّحمن الرّحیم» at the beginning of the Surah symbolizes that the contents of the Surah have been revealed from the Source of Truth and the Manifestation of Mercy.
  2. «بسم اللَّه الرّحمن الرّحیم» at the beginning of the heavenly book means that guidance is achieved only by seeking assistance from Him.(21)
  3. «بسم اللَّه» is the phrase with which the speech of Allah to the people and the speech of the people to Allah begins.
  4. Divine Mercy, like His Essence, is eternal and everlasting. «اللَّه الرّحمن الرّحیم»
  5. Expressing Divine Mercy in various forms is a sign of insistence on mercy. (Both the form of «رحمن» (Rahman) and the form of «رحیم» (Rahim)) «الرّحمن الرّحیم»
  6. Perhaps the inclusion of the words Rahman and Rahim at the beginning of the Book indicates that the Quran is a manifestation of Divine Mercy, just as the origin of creation and the Prophetic mission are manifestations of His grace and mercy. «الرّحمن الرّحیم» 21) Perhaps the meaning of the saying that the entire Quran is summarized in Surah Al-Fatiha, and the entire Surah Al-Fatiha is summarized in Bismillah, and the entire Bismillah is summarized in the letter (Bā'), is that the creation of existence and its guidance are all achieved by seeking assistance from Him. Just as the mission of the Prophet Muhammad صلى الله علیه وآله also began with His Name. «اقرأ باسم ربّك»

Explanations

Portrait of Surah Al-Fatiha Surah Al-Fatiha, whose other name is «فاتحة الكتاب» (The Opening of the Book), has seven Ayahs (1) and is the only Surah that is obligatory for every Muslim to recite ten times daily in the daily prayers, and if intentionally omitted, the prayer is invalid. «لاصلاة الاّ بفاتحة الكتاب»(2) According to the narration of Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله علیه وآله: This Surah is the best of the Surahs of the Quran. And according to Ibn Abbas; Surah Al-Fatiha is the foundation of the Quran. It is also mentioned in a Hadith: If you recite this Surah seventy times over a deceased person and he comes back to life, do not be surprised.(3) The naming of this Surah as «فاتحة الكتاب» by the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله (4) indicates that all the Ayahs of the Quran were compiled during the time of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله علیه وآله and formed into a book, and by His command, this Surah was placed at the beginning and start of the Book (the Quran). Similarly, in the Hadith of Thaqalayn, we read that the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله said: «انّى تارك فیكم الثقلَین كتاب اللَّه و عترتى»(5) I am leaving among you two weighty things: the Book of Allah and my progeny. This Hadith also shows that the Divine Ayahs were compiled in the time of the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله in the form of «كتاب اللّه» (The Book of Allah) and were known and famous among Muslims by this name. The Ayahs of the blessed Surah Al-Fatiha contain references regarding Allah and His attributes, the issue of the Resurrection (Ma'ad), recognition of and request for guidance on the path of truth, and acceptance of the sovereignty and Lordship (Rububiyyah) of Allah. It also expresses affection for continuing the path of Allah's saints and declares aversion and dissociation from those who are astray and those who incurred wrath. Surah Al-Fatiha—like the Quran itself—is a source of healing, both for physical pains and for spiritual illnesses.(6)

Educational Lessons of Surah Al-Fatiha Before interpreting Surah Al-Fatiha, we outline the lessons of this Surah and will elaborate on them in the following pages: 1. By reciting «بسم اللَّه» in Surah Al-Fatiha, man cuts off hope from everything other than Allah. 2. With «ربّ العالمین» (Lord of the worlds) and «مالك یوم الدین» (Master of the Day of Judgment), he feels that he is ruled and owned, and he sets aside selfishness and arrogance. 3. With the word «عالمین» (worlds), he establishes a connection between himself and the entire existence. 4. With «الرّحمن الرّحیم» (The Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful), he knows himself to be under His grace. 5. With «مالك یوم الدین» (Master of the Day of Judgment), his heedlessness regarding the future is removed. 6. By saying «ایّاك نعبد» (You alone we worship), he eliminates hypocrisy and seeking fame. 7. With «ایّاك نستعین» (You alone we ask for help), he does not fear the superpowers. 8. From «انعمت» (You have bestowed favor), he understands that blessings are in His hands. 9. With «اهدنا» (Guide us), he requests to proceed on the path of truth and the straight way. 10. In «صراط الّذین انعمت علیهم» (the path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor), he declares his solidarity with the followers of truth. 11. With «غیر المغضوب علیهم» (not of those who have incurred wrath) and «لا الضّالّین» (nor of those who go astray), he expresses aversion and dissociation from falsehood and the people of falsehood.

1) The number seven is also the number of the heavens, the days of the week, the circumambulation (Tawaf), the running between Safa and Marwah (Sa'y), and the throwing of stones at Satan. 2) Mustadrak, Vol. 4, H. 4365. 3) Bihar, Vol. 92, p. 257. 4) Uyun Akhbar al-Ridha, Vol. 2, p. 27. 5) Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 100. 6) Allamah Amini (may his soul be sanctified) has narrated many traditions in this regard in Tafsir Fatihat al-Kitab.

Ayah

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (۲)
All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds, (2)

Key Points

Rabb (Lord/Sustainer) is applied to one who is both the owner and possessor of something, and who also plays a role in its growth and nurturing.

Allah is both the true Owner of the universe and its Administrator (Mudabbir) and Sustainer (Rabb). Therefore, all existence has an evolutionary movement and is guided along the path that Allah has determined.

In addition to Surah Al-Hamd (The Opening), four other Surahs—Al-An'am, Al-Kahf, Saba', and Fatir—also begin with the phrase "الحمدللّه" (All praise belongs to Allah), but only in Surah Al-Hamd is it followed by "ربّ العالمین" (Lord of the worlds).

The concept of Hamd (praise) is a combination of the concepts of Mad-h (commendation) and Shukr (gratitude). Man offers praise in the face of beauty, perfection, and excellence, and expresses gratitude in return for the blessings, services, and kindness of others. Allah the Almighty is worthy of praise because of His perfection and beauty, and deserving of gratitude because of His kindnesses and blessings.

"الحمدللّه" is the best form of thanking Allah. Anyone, anywhere, in any language, who offers any kind of praise for any perfection or beauty, is in reality praising the Source of that perfection. Of course, praising Allah does not contradict showing gratitude to creation, provided that it is done by the command of Allah and is in line with His path.

Allah is the Sustainer of all creation. "و هو ربّ كلّ شى ء" (And He is the Lord of everything) (22). Whatever is in the heavens and the earth and between them, He is their Sustainer. "ربّ السّموات والارض و مابینهما" (Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them) (23). Imam Ali عليه السلام says: "من الجمادات والحیوانات" (He is the Lord of the inanimate and the animate). "له الخلق والامر تبارك اللّه ربّ العالمین" (To Him belongs the creation and the command. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of the worlds). Both creation and its administration belong to Him, and He is the Educator and Nurturer of all (24).

The meaning of al-’Ālamīn (the worlds) is either exclusively human beings, as in Ayah 70 of Surah Al-Hijr, where the people of Lot said to Prophet Lot: "أوَ لم ننهك عن العالمین" (Did we not forbid you from [dealing with] the worlds/people?), or it refers to all the worlds of existence. ‘Ālam is used to mean creatures, and al-’Ālamīn is also used to mean all creatures. It is understood from this Ayah that all existence has one Sustainer, and the belief held during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and among some nations—that there was a god for every type of phenomenon, whom they considered its administrator (Mudabbir) and Rabb an-Naw’ (Lord of the species)—is false.

22) Al-An'am, 164.

23) Ash-Shu'ara, 24.

24) Tafsir Nur ath-Thaqalayn.

Messages

1- All praises belong to Him. "الحمدللَّه" (25)

2- Allah is not compelled in the nurturing and growth of existence, because praise (Hamd) is for non-compulsory actions. "الحمدللَّه"

3- All existence is beautiful, and the administration of all existence is good, because praise (Hamd) is for beauty and goodness. "الحمدللَّه"

4- The reason for our praise is His Lordship (Sustainer-ship). "الحمدللَّه ربّ العالمین"

5- The relationship between Allah and His creatures is a permanent and close one. "ربّ العالمین" (A painter or builder presents his art and leaves, but a nurturer must supervise every moment.)

6- All existence is under the nurturing of the One God. "ربّ العالمین"

7- The potential for growth and nurturing exists in all beings. "ربّ العالمین"

8- Allah nurtures human beings through the guidance of the prophets (legislative nurturing/ Tarbiyah Tashrī‘ī) and also causes inanimate objects, plants, and animals to grow and develop (creative nurturing/ Tarbiyah Takwīnī). "ربّ العالمین"

9- Believers begin the Book (the Quran) by supplicating to Allah the Almighty, saying, "الحمدللّه ربّ العالمین," and at the end of their journey in Paradise, they give the same declaration: "آخر دعواهم أن الحمدللّه ربّ العالمین" (The conclusion of their prayer will be: All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the worlds) (26).

(25) The Alif and Lām in Al-Hamd signify all praise and the genus of praise.

26) Yunus, 10.

Ayah

ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (۳)
the All-beneficent, the All-merciful, (3)

Key Points

Allah has made mercy incumbent upon Himself, "كتب ربّكم على نفسه الرّحمة" (27), and His mercy encompasses all things. "و رحمتى وسعت كلّ شى ء" (28)

Likewise, the Prophet and His Book are sources of mercy, "رحمةً للعالمین" (29). His creation and nurturing are based on mercy, and even if He inflicts punishment, it is out of grace.

Forgiving sins, accepting the repentance of His servants, concealing their faults, and granting them opportunities to compensate for mistakes—all these are manifestations of His mercy and kindness.

(27) An'am, 54. (28) A'raf, 156. (29) Anbiya, 107.

Messages

1- Divine planning and nurturing are accompanied by love and mercy. (The word "الرحمن" appears alongside the word "ربّ".) "ربّ العالمین الرحمن الرحیم"

2- Just as teaching requires compassion and kindness, "الرّحمن علّم القرآن" (30), nurturing and purification must also be based on compassion and kindness. "ربّ العالمین الرّحمن الرّحیم"

3- Allah's attribute of being the Most Compassionate (Rahmaniyyah) is a reason for praising Him. "الحمدللَّه... الرّحمن الرّحیم"

(30) Ar-Rahman, 1-2.

Ayah

مَـٰلِكِ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٤)
Master of the Day of Retribution. (4)

Key Points

Allah's ownership (Mālikiyyah) is real (Haqīqī), encompassing complete control and sovereignty (Sulṭanah). However, conventional/contractual ownerships (Mālikiyyāt I'tibārī) fall outside the owner's absolute authority and are not under His true sovereignty.

"Māliki Yawm ad-Dīn" (Master of the Day of Judgment). Although Allah is the true Owner of everything at all times, His ownership on the Day of Resurrection (Qiyāmah) and Return (Ma'ād) has a different manifestation:

  • On that Day, all intermediaries and means are severed for the disbelievers. «تقطّعت بهم الاسباب»(31)
  • Relationships and kinship ties vanish for the disbelievers. «فلا انساب بینهم»(32)
  • Wealth, riches, and children are of no benefit to the disbelievers. «لاینفع مال و لابنون»(33)
  • Relatives and close kin also provide no benefit. «لن تنفعكم ارحامكم»(34)
  • Neither the tongues of the disbelievers are permitted to make excuses, nor do their minds have the opportunity for planning. The only recourse is the grace of Allah, Who is the Master of Authority on that Day.

The word Dīn has been used in various meanings: A: The collection of divine laws. «إنّ الدّین عنداللَّه الاسلام»(35) B: Action and obedience. «للّه الدّین الخالص»(36) C: Reckoning and recompense (Jazā'). «مالك یوم الدّین»

"Yawm ad-Dīn" in the Quran means the Day of Resurrection (Qiyāmah), which is the day of punishment and reward. «یسئلون ایّان یوم الدّین»(37) They ask, "When is the Day of Judgment?"

In introducing this Day, the Quran states: «ثم ما ادراك ما یوم الدّین . یوم لا تملك نفس شیئاً و الامر یومئذ للّه»(38) (O Prophet!) Do you know what the Day of Judgment is? A Day when no soul shall have power for another, and the command on that Day belongs solely to Allah.

"Māliki Yawm ad-Dīn" is a type of warning (Inzār) and caution (Hushdār), but by being placed alongside the verse «الرّحمن الرّحیم», it becomes clear that good tidings (Bashārah) and warning must be presented together.

Similar to another noble verse which states: «نبّى ء عبادى انّى أنا الغفور الرّحیم . و أنّ عذابى هو العذاب الالیم»(39) Inform My servants that I am the Forgiving, the Merciful, but My punishment and retribution is also the painful punishment.

Likewise, in another verse, He introduces Himself thus: «قابل التَّوب شدید العقاب»(40) Allah is the Acceptor of repentance from people and the Severe Punisher of sinners.

In the first Surah of the Quran, Allah's ownership (Mālikiyyah) is mentioned, «مالكِ یوم الدّین», and in the last Surah, His kingship (Malikiyyah) is mentioned. «ملك النّاس»

31) Baqarah, 166. 32) Mu'minūn, 101. 33) Shu'arā', 88. 34) Mumtaḥanah, 3. 35) Āl 'Imrān, 19. 36) Zumar, 3. 37) Dhāriyāt, 12. 38) Infiṭār, 18 - 19. 39) Ḥijr, 49 - 50. 40) Ghāfir, 3.

Messages

1- Almighty Allah is worthy of worship from various perspectives, and we must offer Him praise and thanks: because of the perfection of His Essence and Attributes, which is "Allah"; because of His benevolence and nurturing, which is "Rabb al-'Ālamīn" (Lord of the Worlds); because of the hope and expectation of His mercy and grace, which is "Ar-Raḥmān Ar-Raḥīm" (The Most Compassionate, the Ever Merciful); and because of His power and awe, which is "Māliki Yawm ad-Dīn" (Master of the Day of Judgment).

2- The Resurrection (Qiyāmah) is a manifestation of His Lordship (Rubūbiyyah). «ربّ العالمین... مالك یوم الدین»

3- The Resurrection (Qiyāmah) is a manifestation of Allah's Mercy. «الرّحمن الرّحیم مالك یوم الدین»

Ayah

إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ (٥)
You [alone] do we worship, and to You [alone] do we turn for help. (5)

Key Points

Man must accept the servitude (worship) of Allah based on the judgment of reason (intellect). We humans are lovers of perfection and are in need of growth and nurturing, and Allah is the possessor of all perfections and the Lord (Rabb) of all existence. If we are in need of kindness and affection, He is al-Rahman (the Most Compassionate) and al-Rahim (the Most Merciful); and if we are worried about the distant future, He is the Master and Owner (Malik) of that Day. So why should we turn to others?! Reason dictates that we must worship only Him and seek help only from Him. We should be neither a slave to our own whims and desires nor a slave to the wealth and power of others.

In prayer (Salat), it is as if the worshipper, representing all monotheists, says: O Allah! Not only I, but all of us are Your servants, and not only I, but all of us are in need and dependent on Your grace.

O Allah! I have no one but You (Iyyāka), but You have many besides me, and all existence is Your servant and slave. «ان كلّ من فى السموات و الارض الاّ اتى الرّحمن عبداً» (41) There is nothing in the heavens and the earth except that it comes to the Most Compassionate (Allah) as a servant.

The phrase na‘budu (we worship) indicates both that the prayer should be performed in congregation and that Muslims are all brothers and on the same path.

The stages of spiritual ascent consist of: praise (thana), connection, and then supplication (du‘a). Therefore, the beginning of Surah Al-Hamd is praise, the verse «ایّاك نعبد» is connection, and the subsequent verses are supplication.

Conversation with the true Beloved is sweet; perhaps this is why the word Iyyāka was repeated. (41) Maryam, 93.

Messages

1- First, one must perform servitude to Allah, and then ask Him for needs. «نعبد، نستعین» (We worship, we seek help).

2- Servitude is permissible only before Allah, not others. «ایّاك نعبد» (You alone we worship).

3- Although worship comes from us, we are still in need of His help even in performing that worship. «ایّاك نعبد و ایّاك نستعین» (42)

4- «ایّاك نعبد و ایّاك نستعین» means neither compulsion (jabr) nor delegation (tafwid). Since we say na‘budu (we worship), we possess free will and are not compelled. And since we say nasta‘in (we seek help), we are in need of Him, and affairs have not been delegated to us.

5- Knowing Allah and His attributes is the prerequisite for achieving Tawhid (monotheism) and the worship of the One God. «ربّ العالمین، الرّحمن الرّحیم، مالك یوم الدّین، ایّاك نعبد»

6- Among the manners of supplication and worship is that man should not put himself forward but should feel himself in the presence of Allah. «ایّاك نعبد...»

7- Attention to the Resurrection (Ma‘ad) is one of the motivations for worship. «مالك یوم الدّین ایّاك نعبد» (42) «و ما كنا لنهتدى لولا ان هدانا اللَّه» Had it not been for Allah’s guidance, we would not have been guided. A‘raf, 43.

Ayah

ٱهْدِنَا ٱلصِّرَٰطَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ (٦)
Guide us on the straight path, (6)

Key Points

Two types of guidance are presented in the Holy Quran:

A: Takwini (Cosmic) Guidance, such as the guidance of the honeybee on how to suck the nectar from flowers and how to build a hive. Or the guidance and direction of birds in their winter and summer migrations. Where the Quran states: «ربّنا الّذى اعطى كلّ شى ء خلقه ثم هدى» (43) (Our Lord is He Who gave to each thing its creation, then guided it), it expresses this type of guidance.

B: Tashri'i (Legislative) Guidance, which is the sending of the Divine Prophets and heavenly books for the guidance of humanity.

The word Sirat (Path) (44) has appeared more than forty times in the Quran. Choosing the correct path and intellectual line is a sign of human character.

Multiple non-Divine paths are placed before man, and he must choose one:

  • The path of one's own desires and expectations.
  • The path of people's expectations and whims.
  • The path of Satan's temptations.
  • The path of the tyrants (Taghuts).
  • The path of ancestors and predecessors.
  • The path of Allah and the friends of Allah (Awliya).

The believing person chooses the path of Allah and His friends, which has advantages over other paths:

A: The Divine path is constant, unlike the paths of tyrants, people's whims, and personal desires, which change every day.

B: It is only one path, while other paths are multiple and scattered.

C: In traversing it, man is certain of the route and the destination.

D: In traversing it, there is no failure or loss.

  • The Straight Path is the path of Allah. «انّ ربّى على صراطٍ مستقیم» (45)
  • The Straight Path is the path of the Prophets. «انّك لَمِن المرسلین على صراطٍ مستقیم» (46)
  • The Straight Path is the path of servitude to Allah. «و أنِ اعبدونى هذا صراط مستقیم» (47)
  • The Straight Path is reliance and dependence upon Allah. «مَن یعتصم باللّه فقد هُدىَ الى صراط مستقیم» (48)
  • The Straight Path is monotheism and seeking help only from Him. (49)
  • The Straight Path is the Book of Allah. (50)
  • The Straight Path is the path of sound innate nature (Fitrah). (51)

Man must seek help from Allah both in choosing the Straight Path and in continuing upon it. Like a lamp that receives its light from the power station every moment. «اهدنا الصّراط المستقیم» (Guide us to the Straight Path).

Being on the Straight Path is the only request that every Muslim asks of Allah in every prayer. Even the Messenger of Allah صلى الله علیه وآله and the infallible Imams علیهم السلام ask Allah for steadfastness on the Straight Path.

Man must always ask Allah for the Straight Path in every type of his affairs, whether it be choosing a job, a friend, a field of study, or a spouse. Because one might think correctly in beliefs but stumble in action, or vice versa. Therefore, asking Allah for the Straight Path every moment is essential.

The Straight Path has ranks and stages. Even those who are on the path of truth, like the friends of Allah, need to pray for remaining on the path and for the increase of the light of guidance. «والّذین اهتدوا زادَهم هُدى» (52)

The Straight Path is the middle and central way, about which Hadrat Ali عليه السلام says: «الیمین و الشمال مضلّة و الطریق الوسطى هى الجادّة» (53) Deviation to the left and right is misguidance, and the middle path is the highway (of guidance).

The Straight Path means moderation and temperance, and avoiding all types of excess (Ifrat) and negligence (Tafrit), whether in belief or action. One deviates from the path in beliefs, and another in action and ethics. One attributes all actions to Allah, as if man has no role in his own destiny. And another considers himself the sole actor and doer of whatever he wills, believing the Hand of Allah is tied. One introduces the heavenly leaders as ordinary people, sometimes as sorcerers or madmen, and another considers those great figures to be on the level of God. One considers visiting the infallible Imams and martyrs an innovation (Bid'ah), and another even ties ropes and seeks intercession through trees and walls. One considers economics the infrastructure, and another disregards the world and its affairs.

In action, too, one has misplaced zeal (Ghayrat), and another sends his wife unveiled into the streets and markets. One is stingy, and another spends generously without calculation. One separates himself from creation, and another sacrifices the Truth for the sake of creation.

Such behaviors and actions are deviations from the Straight Path of guidance. Allah introduces His firm and stable religion as the Straight Path. (54) It is narrated in traditions that the infallible Imams علیهم السلام used to say: We are the Straight Path. (55) Meaning, the heavenly leaders are the tangible and practical examples of the Straight Path, and the role models (Uswah) for stepping onto the path. In their instructions regarding all matters of life, such as work, recreation, education, nutrition, spending (Infaq), criticism, estrangement, reconciliation, and affection for children, etc., they have given opinions and advised us toward temperance and moderation. (56) It is interesting that Iblis lies in wait on this very Straight Path. (57)

Many examples are given in the Quran and traditions where moderation is emphasized and excess and negligence are forbidden. Pay attention to the following cases:

  • «كلوا و اشربوا و لاتسرفوا» (58) Eat and drink, but do not commit excess (Israf).
  • «لا تَجعل یدك مَغلولةً الى عُنقك و لاتبسُطها كلّ البَسط» (59) In spending, neither chain your hand to your neck—be tight-fisted—nor extend it with total extension, such that you yourself become needy.
  • «الّذین اذا أنفقوا لم یسرفوا و لم یقتروا و كان بین ذلك قواماً» (60) Believers, when they spend, are neither extravagant nor stingy, but maintain a balance between the two.
  • «لا تجهر بصلاتك و لاتخافت بها وابتغ بین ذلك سبیلاً» (61) Do not recite your prayer loudly nor softly, but seek a way between that.
  • Be kind to parents; «و بالوالدین احساناً» (62) but if they strive to make you deviate from the path of Allah, obedience to them is not necessary. «ان جاهداك على ان تشرك بى... فلاتطعهما» (63)
  • The Prophet has both a general mission; «و كان رسولاً نبیّاً» (64) and invites his own family. «و كان یأمر أهله بالصّلاة» (65)
  • Islam recommends both prayer (Salat), which is connection with the Creator; «اقیموا الصّلاة» and recommends Zakat, which is connection with the people. «اتوا الزكاة» (66)
  • Neither should affections divert you from bearing witness to the truth; «شهداء لِلّه و لو على انفسكم» (67) nor should enmities distance you from justice. «و لا یَجرِمنّكم شَنئان قوم» (68)
  • Believers have both repulsion; «أشداء على الكفار» and attraction. «رحماء بینهم» (69)
  • Both faith and heartfelt conviction are necessary; «امنوا» and righteous deeds. «عملوا الصالحات» (70)
  • Both tears, supplication, and asking Allah for victory are necessary; «ربّنا افرغ علینا صبراً» (71) and patience and steadfastness in hardships. «عشرون صابرون یغلبوا مأتین» (72) On the night of Ashura, Imam Husayn عليه السلام both supplicated and sharpened his sword.
  • On the Day of Arafah and the night of Eid al-Adha, the pilgrim of the House of Allah recites supplications, and on the Day of Eid, he must become acquainted with blood at the place of sacrifice.
  • Islam accepts ownership, «الناس مسلّطون على اموالهم» (73) but does not permit harming others and restricts it. «لا ضَرر و لا ضِرار» (74)

Yes, Islam is not a one-dimensional religion that only focuses on one aspect and forgets other dimensions, but rather, in every matter, it recommends moderation, temperance, and the Straight Path.

(43) Taha, 50. (44) Sirat in the Hereafter is the name of a bridge over Hell that all people must cross. (45) Hud, 56. (46) Ya Sin, 3-4. (47) Ya Sin, 61. (48) Al Imran, 101. (49) Based on the fact that the Alif and Lam in «الصراط» refer to the same path of monotheism mentioned in the previous verse. (50) Based on a narration in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 1, p. 58. (51) Based on a narration from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام in Tafsir Safi, Vol. 1, p. 86. (52) Whoever says «الحمدللَّه ربّ العالمین الرّحمن الرّحیم...» has passed through stages of guidance; therefore, his request is for guidance to a higher stage. (53) Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 87, p. 3. (54) «قل انّنى هَدانى ربّى الى صراط مستقیم» (Say: Indeed, my Lord has guided me to a Straight Path) An'am, 161. (55) Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 20. (56) Regarding this, one can refer to the book Usul al-Kafi, Chapter on Moderation in Worship (Bab al-Iqtisad fi al-Ibadat). (57) Satan said to Allah: «لاقعدنّ لهم صراطك المستقیم» (I will surely sit in wait for them on Your Straight Path) A'raf, 16. (58) A'raf, 31. (59) Isra, 29. (60) Furqan, 67. (61) Isra, 110. (62) Baqarah, 83. (63) Luqman, 15. (64) Maryam, 51. (65) Maryam, 55. (66) Baqarah, 43. (67) Nisa, 135. (68) Ma'idah, 8. (69) Fath, 29. (70) Baqarah, 25. (71) Baqarah, 250. (72) Anfal, 65. (73) Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 2, p. 272. (74) Kafi, Vol. 5, p. 28.

Messages

  1. All existence moves in the path willed by Allah. O God! Place us too on the path that You love. «اهدنا الصراط المستقیم»
  2. Requesting guidance to the Straight Path is the most important request of monotheists. «ایّاك نعبد... اهدنا الصراط المستقیم»
  3. To attain the Straight Path, one must supplicate. «اهدنا الصراط المستقیم»
  4. First praise, then seeking help and supplication. «الحمدللَّه... اهدنا»
  5. The best example of seeking help from Allah is the request for the Straight Path. «ایّاك نستعین اهدنا اهدنا الصّراط المستقیم»

Ayah

صِرَٰطَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ ٱلْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (۷)
the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray. (7)

Key Points

This verse introduces the straight path as the path of those upon whom Allah has bestowed favor (75). These are the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous. Paying attention to the path of these great figures, desiring to tread it, and constantly reminding oneself of this desire, protects us from the danger of deviation and falling into misguided lines. Following this request, one asks Allah not to place him on the path of those who incurred wrath or the astray. This is because the Children of Israel, according to the Quran, were also favored with blessings, but due to ingratitude and obstinacy, they incurred wrath.

The Quran divides people into three categories: those who received the blessing of guidance and remained steadfast, those who incurred wrath, and the astray.

The meaning of "blessing" (نعمت) in «انعمتَ علیهم» (You have bestowed favor upon them) is the blessing of guidance. This is because the previous verse spoke of guidance. Furthermore, material blessings are also possessed by disbelievers, deviants, and others.

Even the guided are at risk, and we must constantly ask Allah that our path does not lead to wrath or straying.

Those who incurred Wrath in the Quran

In the Quran, individuals like Pharaoh, Qarun, and Abu Lahab, and nations such as the people of 'Ad, Thamud, and the Children of Israel, are identified as those who incurred wrath (76).

The Children of Israel, whose life and civilization story is narrated in the Quran, once held superiority over the people of their time: «فَضّلتُكم على العالَمین» (77). However, after this distinction and superiority, due to their own behavior, they incurred the wrath and punishment of Allah. «و باؤوا بغضب من اللّه» (78). This change in destiny was due to a change in their behavior and actions: Jewish scholars distorted the heavenly commands and laws of the Torah, «یحرّفون الكلم» (79); their merchants and wealthy individuals turned to usury, illicit gain, and luxury-seeking, «اخذهم الرّبا» (80); and the common people, out of laziness and fear, refused to go to the battlefield and enter the Holy Land when called to Jihad and struggle. «فاذهب انت و ربّك فقاتلا انّا هاهُنا قاعدون» (81). Because of these deviations, Allah afflicted them, bringing them from the height of honor and distinction to the depths of humiliation and disgrace.

In every prayer, we ask Allah not to be like those who incurred wrath—meaning, neither those who distort verses, nor those involved in usury, nor those who flee from Jihad in the way of truth. Similarly, we ask not to be among the astray, those who abandon the truth and pursue falsehood, who exaggerate or commit excess in their religion and beliefs, or who follow their own desires or the desires of others (82).

In this Surah, man expresses his love, affection, and loyalty for the prophets, martyrs, and the righteous, and their path, while also declaring disassociation and distance from those who incurred wrath and the astray throughout history. This is an example of Tawalli (loyalty) and Tabarri (disassociation).

The Astray in the Quran

The word Dhalalat (straying), which appears about two hundred times with its derivatives in the Quran, is sometimes used for confusion or bewilderment, «و وَجدَك ضالاً» (83), and sometimes it means being wasted or lost, «اضلّ اعمالهم» (84). However, it mostly means straying or going astray, and is seen with various expressions such as: Dhalal Mubin (manifest straying), Dhalal Ba'id (far straying), and Dhalal Kabir (great straying).

In the Quran, certain individuals are identified as astray, including: those who exchanged their faith for disbelief (85), polytheists (86), disbelievers (87), transgressors (88), Muslims who took disbelievers as guardians and friends (89), those who hinder people from the path of Allah, those who insult Allah or His Messenger, those who conceal the truth, and those who despair of Allah's Mercy.

In the Quran, some are named as misleaders, such as: Iblis, Pharaoh, Samiri, bad friends, and deviant leaders and ancestors.

The astray themselves prepare the ground and context for deviation, and the misleaders utilize these ready contexts and conditions. The contexts for deviation mentioned in the Quran are: 1. Desires (90), 2. Idols (91), 3. Sins (92), 4. Accepting false guardianship (93), 5. Ignorance and foolishness (94).

75) «من یطع اللّه والرّسول فاولئك مع الّذین انعم اللّه علیهم من النبیّین والصدّیقین والشهداء والصالحین», Those who obey Allah and the Messenger, they are with those upon whom Allah has bestowed favor, from among the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous. Surah An-Nisa, 69 and Surah Maryam, 59.

76) In numerous verses of the Quran, the characteristics and examples of the astray and those who incurred wrath are explained. The following are examples: * Hypocrites, polytheists, and those who harbor evil thoughts about Allah. Surah An-Nisa, 116 and Surah Al-Fath, 6. * Disbelievers in the divine verses and killers of Allah's prophets. Surah Al-Baqarah, 61. * The People of the Book who rebelled against the call to truth. Surah Aal-i-Imran, 110 - 112. * Those who flee from Jihad. Surah Al-Anfal, 16. * Those who accept and replace faith with disbelief. Surah Al-Baqarah, 108 and Surah An-Nahl, 106. * Those who accept the guardianship of Allah's enemies and those who love relations with Allah's enemies. Surah Al-Mumtahanah, 1.

82) «قل یا أهل الكتاب لاتَغلوا فى دینكم غیرالحقّ ولاتتّبعوا أهواءقوم قدضَلّوا من قبل وأضلّوا عن سواءالسبیل» Say: O People of the Book! Do not commit excess in your religion beyond the truth, and do not follow the desires of a people who went astray before and led many astray, and strayed from the straight path. Surah Al-Ma'idah, 77.

83) Surah Ad-Duha, 7.

84) Surah Muhammad, 1.

85) «من یَتبدّل الكفر بالایمان فقد ضلّ سواء السبیل» Whoever exchanges disbelief for faith has certainly strayed from the straight path. Surah Al-Baqarah, 108.

86) «و من یشرك باللَّه فقد ضل ضلالا بعیدا» And whoever associates partners with Allah has certainly strayed far away. Surah An-Nisa, 116.

87) «و من یكفر باللَّه... فقد ضلّ» And whoever disbelieves in Allah... has certainly strayed. Surah An-Nisa, 136.

88) «و من یعص اللَّه و رسوله فقد ضل» And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly strayed. Surah Al-Ahzab, 36.

89) «لا تتخذوا عدوى و عدوكم اولیاء... و من یفعله منكم فقد ضل سواء السبیل» Do not take My enemy and your enemy as allies... and whoever among you does that has certainly strayed from the straight path. Surah Al-Mumtahanah, 1.

90) «اتّخذ الهه هَواه واضلّه اللَّه» He took his desire as his god, and Allah led him astray. Surah Al-Jathiyah, 23.

91) «جعلوا لِلّه انداداً لِیضلّوا عن سبیله» They set up rivals to Allah to lead [people] astray from His path. Surah Ibrahim, 30.

92) «وما یضلّ به الا الفاسقین» And He does not lead astray thereby except the defiantly disobedient. Surah Al-Baqarah, 26.

93) «انّه من تولاّه فانّه یضلّه» Indeed, whoever takes him (Satan) as a guardian, he will certainly lead him astray. Surah Al-Hajj, 4.

94) «و ان كنتم من قبله لمن الضّالّین» And indeed, you were before it among those astray. Surah Al-Baqarah, 198.

Messages

1- Man requires role models in education and training. The prophets, martyrs, the truthful, and the righteous are beautiful examples of humanity. «صراط الّذین انعمت علیهم»

2- Whatever reaches man from Allah is a blessing. We ourselves create wrath and punishment (95). «انعمت، المغضوب علیهم»

3- Expressing aversion to those who incurred wrath and the astray makes the Islamic society resilient and steadfast against accepting their governance. «غیر المغضوب علیهم ولا الضّالّین» (96)

95) The word «أنعَمتَ» (You bestowed favor) is used for blessing, but concerning punishment, He did not say «غَضبتَ» (You became wrathful).

96) The Quran advises: «لا تَتولّوا قوماً غَضِب اللّه علیهم» Never accept the guardianship of a people upon whom Allah is wrathful. Surah Al-Mumtahanah, 13.
